content.text: Incident Commander Role and Roster Management

During an incident, the most common failure mode is not technical—it is organizational. Multiple engineers simultaneously debug different hypotheses, no one owns external communication, decisions get made and reversed without coordination, and the resolution takes twice as long as the technical complexity warrants. The Incident Commander (IC) role exists to prevent exactly this. The IC does not fix the system; they create the conditions under which the technical team can fix the system as fast as possible.
The IC Org Structure

attrs.alt: Incident commander role structure and responsibilities

content.text: What the IC Does Not Do

The IC does not debug the system, write code, or run commands on production servers. The moment the IC reaches for a keyboard to fix a problem, they have abandoned their role and left the team without coordination. The IC's job is to keep the response organized so that the engineers who are debugging can work without interruption.
Does not debug or write mitigation code. Delegates all technical execution to the Technical Lead.
Does not answer every question in the incident channel. The IC speaks to coordinate, not to narrate.
Does not make technical decisions unilaterally. The IC endorses and implements the Technical Lead's recommendations.
Responsibilities by Phase

Declare (T+0 to T+5): Open the incident Slack channel (#inc-YYYY-MM-DD-NNN), page the Technical Lead and Scribe, assign severity and category, link the correct runbook, and post the first status page update.

Coordinate (T+5 to mitigation): Run 5-minute bridge call updates, remove blockers between teams, gate any new deployments that could interfere, track elapsed time against SLA targets, and escalate severity when new evidence warrants it.

Close (after mitigation): Confirm mitigation with the Technical Lead, wait 30 minutes of stable metrics before declaring all-clear, post the final status page update, assign the postmortem owner, and hand off action items.
Roster Configuration

The IC roster must exist before any incident. A roster that is assembled during an incident wastes the first critical minutes and produces unclear accountability. The roster defines who can serve as IC, what their training certification requires, and how primary and backup rotation is scheduled.

# ic-roster.yaml
roster:
  version: "2.0"
  rotation_schedule: weekly  # Each IC serves for one calendar week
  minimum_certified_ics: 6   # Minimum in roster before understaffing alert

  certification:
    required_training:
      - ic-fundamentals-course  # 4-hour async course
      - tabletop-simulation     # Live scenario with a senior IC observer
      - shadow-shift            # 1 week shadowing a certified IC
      - solo-shift-observed     # First solo week with senior IC on standby
    recertification_months: 12
    disqualifying_absence_days: 90  # IC cert lapses after 90 days off-roster

  members:
    - id: alice.chen
      name: Alice Chen
      team: ai-platform
      cert_date: 2025-11-01
      availability: primary  # Can be paged as primary IC
      specializations: [capability_failures, llm_infra]

    - id: bob.kumar
      name: Bob Kumar
      team: sre
      cert_date: 2025-09-15
      availability: primary
      specializations: [infrastructure, network]

    - id: carol.james
      name: Carol James
      team: trust-and-safety
      cert_date: 2025-10-20
      availability: backup  # Can be paged only if primary unavailable
      specializations: [safety_incidents, compliance]

    - id: dan.okonkwo
      name: Dan Okonkwo
      team: security
      cert_date: 2026-01-05
      availability: primary
      specializations: [security_incidents, prompt_injection]

  escalation_path:
    - level: 1
      role: primary_ic
      response_time_minutes: 5
    - level: 2
      role: backup_ic
      response_time_minutes: 10
    - level: 3
      role: engineering_director
      response_time_minutes: 15
      trigger: sev1_only

  pagerduty:
    service_id: PD_IC_ROTATION
    escalation_policy: IC_ESCALATION_POLICY
    rotation_type: weekly_on_call
IC Handoff Protocol During an Active Incident

Long incidents require IC handoffs. An IC managing an incident for more than 2 hours begins to make errors of omission and coordination failures from fatigue. The handoff protocol ensures continuity without dropping context.

# IC Handoff Template

## Incident ID: inc-2026-07-14-003
## Outgoing IC: Alice Chen
## Incoming IC: Bob Kumar
## Handoff Time: 2026-07-14 10:30 UTC

### Current Status
- **Severity:** SEV-2
- **Category:** Infrastructure / Rate Limit Exhaustion
- **Impact:** 15% of requests returning HTTP 429 to end users
- **Duration:** 87 minutes

### What We Know
- LLM provider hit our daily token quota at 09:03 UTC
- Root cause: overnight batch job consumed 40% of daily quota
- Mitigation in progress: switching 50% of traffic to backup provider

### What We Don't Know
- Whether backup provider can sustain load at current rate
- ETA to full mitigation (estimate: 20 minutes)

### Active Tasks
- [ ] Dan implementing traffic split (owner: dan.okonkwo, ETA: 10:45)
- [ ] Carol updating status page at T+30 cadence (next: 10:50)

### Pending Decisions
- If backup provider also rate-limits: halt batch jobs or enforce per-user quotas?

### Key Contacts
- LLM provider TAM: vendor-tam@provider.com (paged, awaiting response)
- Engineering director: notified via Slack, monitoring

### Next Update Cadence
- Bridge call: every 5 minutes
- Status page: every 30 minutes
- Exec ping: at 11:00 if not mitigated
Roster Health Automation

The IC roster is a safety system. Automated checks should alert roster owners before it becomes understaffed or before certifications lapse. The following script generates a daily roster health report.
IC Specialization and Category Routing

The IC role is generalist by design—the IC coordinates rather than debugging. However, for safety and security incidents, routing the page to an IC with specialization in those categories accelerates the critical first 5 minutes because they know exactly which notifications to send and which escalation contacts to reach.
Incident Category
Preferred IC Specialization
If Unavailable
Capability
AI Platform background
Any certified IC
Infrastructure
SRE background
Any certified IC
Safety
Trust and Safety or Legal awareness
Page T&S team in parallel
Security
Security engineering background
Page Security team immediately regardless
IC Authority and Decision Rights

The IC role only functions if the organization genuinely grants the IC decision-making authority during incidents. Authority that must be re-negotiated during the incident is not authority—it is a suggestion.

The IC has unilateral authority to: halt all non-emergency deployments site-wide, demand engineering resources from any team, override normal change-freeze approval processes for emergency mitigations, and declare all-clear. This authority expires when the incident is declared resolved. Document these decision rights in an org-level policy and review them with engineering leadership annually.
On-Call Schedule and Coverage Windows

The IC on-call schedule must provide 24/7 coverage with no gaps. In practice, this requires a minimum of six certified ICs to maintain a weekly rotation while accounting for vacations, sick leave, and training blackout periods. The schedule should be published at least four weeks in advance and integrated with the broader engineering on-call calendar to avoid conflicts where the same person is primary IC and primary SRE on the same week.

"""ic_schedule.py -- weekly IC rotation generator.

Generates a 12-week IC rotation from the roster, respecting
blackout dates and maximum consecutive on-call weeks per person.
"""
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, timedelta
from typing import Optional
import yaml


@dataclass
class ShiftAssignment:
    week_start: date
    primary_ic: str
    backup_ic: str
    notes: str = ""


def generate_rotation(
    roster_path: str = "ic-roster.yaml",
    start_date: Optional[date] = None,
    weeks: int = 12,
) -> list[ShiftAssignment]:
    """Generate IC rotation, avoiding back-to-back assignments."""
    with open(roster_path) as f:
        roster_data = yaml.safe_load(f)["roster"]

    # Only certified primaries for primary slot
    primaries = [
        m["id"] for m in roster_data["members"]
        if m["availability"] == "primary"
    ]
    backups = [
        m["id"] for m in roster_data["members"]
        if m["availability"] in ("primary", "backup")
    ]

    if len(primaries) < 2:
        raise ValueError("Need at least 2 primary ICs for rotation")

    if start_date is None:
        # Start on the next Monday
        today = date.today()
        start_date = today + timedelta(days=(7 - today.weekday()) % 7)

    assignments = []
    last_primary = None
    last_backup = None

    for week_num in range(weeks):
        week_start = start_date + timedelta(weeks=week_num)

        # Rotate through primaries, skip last assigned
        for candidate in primaries:
            if candidate != last_primary:
                primary = candidate
                break
        else:
            primary = primaries[0]  # fallback if all tried

        # Backup must differ from primary and last backup
        for candidate in backups:
            if candidate != primary and candidate != last_backup:
                backup = candidate
                break
        else:
            backup = backups[0]

        assignments.append(ShiftAssignment(
            week_start=week_start,
            primary_ic=primary,
            backup_ic=backup,
        ))
        last_primary = primary
        last_backup = backup

    return assignments


if __name__ == "__main__":
    rotation = generate_rotation(weeks=8)
    print(f"IC Rotation Schedule ({len(rotation)} weeks)")
    print("-" * 50)
    for shift in rotation:
        print(
            f"{shift.week_start}  Primary: {shift.primary_ic:<20}"
            f"Backup: {shift.backup_ic}"
        )
IC Training and Certification Process

Certification is a gate, not a formality. An engineer who takes the async course but has never handled a live incident under observation is not ready to command. The certification path is deliberately sequential: each stage builds on the previous and cannot be skipped.

IC Fundamentals Course (4 hours): async course covering the IC role definition, decision tree, communication templates, escalation paths, and common failure modes. Assessed by a written quiz with a passing score of 85%.

Tabletop Simulation (3 hours): live scenario run by a senior IC trainer, simulating a SEV-2 safety incident with injected complications (conflicting information, unavailable team members, executive pressure). Assessed on decision quality and communication clarity.

Shadow Shift (1 week): the candidate shadows a certified IC during their on-call week, attending all incident bridge calls, reviewing all communications before they are sent, and debriefing after every incident or drill.

Solo Shift Observed (1 week): the candidate serves as primary IC while a certified IC mentor is on standby but does not intervene unless the candidate requests help or makes a decision that would cause significant harm.

Annual recertification requires one tabletop simulation refresher per year. ICs who have not commanded an incident in the past 90 days are required to complete the shadow shift again before returning to the primary rotation.
Key Takeaways
The IC coordinates rather than debugs. The moment an IC starts typing commands on production systems, the team loses its coordinator and incident duration increases.
Roster health is a safety property. Automate certification expiry checks and understaffing alerts so the roster never fails at the moment it is needed most.
IC handoffs during long incidents require a structured handoff document. Context transferred informally via chat is context lost.
The IC's authority must be documented and endorsed by engineering leadership before incidents occur. Contested authority during an incident is organizationally equivalent to no authority.
For safety and security incidents, prefer routing to an IC with domain familiarity to accelerate the critical first-five-minute notification sequence.
